column2: The Tennessee Valley Authority (TVA) proposes to build and operate the Yellow Creek Nuclear Plant, consisting of two pressurized water reactor units on a 1,160-acre site along the Yellow Creek embayment of Pickwick Lake in Tishomingo County, Mississippi, about nine miles north of Iuka. Each unit has a net electrical output of about 1,285-1,339 MW, with heat dissipated through two natural-draft cooling towers and blowdown discharged to the Tennessee River. The project also includes new highway and rail access, an intake on Pickwick Lake, and roughly 120 miles of new 500-kV and 161-kV transmiss…
column7: The summary sheet and Introduction visible in the pull confirm the core facts: 2-unit nuclear plant in Tishomingo County MS, March 1985/1986 commercial operation dates, nuclear chosen over coal on co…

column2: The site is in a rural, primarily agricultural and forested area, with about 6,125 people living within 10 miles, including the small towns of Iuka and Burnsville, Mississippi, and Waterloo, Alabama. Larger nearby communities within 50 miles include Corinth, Mississippi, and the Florence-Muscle Shoals-Sheffield-Tuscumbia area in Alabama, and the Yellow Creek embayment is heavily used for recreation, with about 3.37 million visits in 1974. Construction would bring up to 2,600 workers at peak, adding around 470 students to local schools and increasing demand for housing, water and sewer service…

TVA evaluated taking no action, delaying the plant, purchasing power, reactivating older facilities, and using existing peaking units as base load, as well as new generation from hydroelectric, geothermal, natural gas, oil, coal, and nuclear sources. Nuclear was chosen over high-sulfur coal—the next cheapest option—based on an estimated $126 million per year cost advantage and lower environmental impacts, while energy conservation was incorporated into demand forecasts and solar and municipal solid waste were judged not yet practical at this scale. Two candidate sites were compared, Yellow Cr…

Routine operations are expected to release radioactive, chemical, and heated discharges within regulatory limits, with radiation doses to nearby populations a small fraction of the natural background of about 115-150 mrem per year. Cooling tower operation will evaporate water, add small amounts of drift and heat, and slightly raise discharge temperatures, while entrainment could affect 7-10 percent of larval fish in the Yellow Creek embayment, with negligible effects on the wider reservoir. Construction will clear about 440 acres at the site and disturb roughly 3,985 acres overall (including …

TVA circulated the draft statement in May 1977 and the final statement in January 1978 to federal agencies (including EPA, CEQ, Interior, Agriculture, HUD, and ERDA) and to state and local agencies in Mississippi, Tennessee, and Alabama. The Tennessee State Planning Office and Hardin County officials requested stronger commitments to mitigate impacts on schools, water and sewer systems, recreation, ambulance, law enforcement, and civil defense, and asked about cumulative effects with the Tennessee-Tombigbee Waterway and Pickwick Lock projects.
